title: SEGMENT 5 — The Human Microbiome Project and the catalog era

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — The Human Microbiome Project and the catalog era
In 2007, with the cost of sequencing dropping rapidly and the 16S workflow becoming standardized, the U.
S.
National Institutes of Health launched an enormous coordinated effort called the Human Microbiome Project, or HMP.
It was modeled, in a way, on the Human Genome Project — a large, well-funded, multi-center effort to produce a foundational reference dataset that the rest of the field could build on.
The Human Genome Project gave us the sequence of one (composite) human.
The Human Microbiome Project was supposed to give us a catalog of the microbes that lived in and on humans, sampled across body sites and people, sequenced deeply, and made publicly available.
The HMP ran in two phases.

Phase one, from 2007 to 2014, sampled around three hundred healthy adults at five major body sites — gut (via stool), skin (multiple locations), oral cavity (multiple locations including tongue, gum, and tooth surfaces), respiratory tract (nasal passages), and urogenital tract (vagina, in women).
At each site, they did 16S sequencing for hundreds of samples and shotgun metagenomics for a strategically selected subset.
The output was a reference catalog of the human microbiome in apparent health — what bacteria and archaea live where, in what abundances, and with what variability between people.

Phase two, from 2014 to 2016, called the Integrative Human Microbiome Project or iHMP, focused on three specific disease cohorts — pregnancy and preterm birth, inflammatory bowel disease, and prediabetes — and tracked microbiome dynamics longitudinally over time alongside host molecular measurements.
The findings from HMP phase one, published in a flurry of papers in 2012, established several foundational facts about the human microbiome.
First, every body site has its own distinct microbial community.

The bacteria on your tongue are different from the bacteria between your teeth, which are different from the bacteria in your gut, which are different from the bacteria on your skin, which differ between your forehead and your forearm.
The composition is so distinctive that you can pretty reliably guess which body site a sample came from just by looking at its microbiome profile.
Second, there is enormous person-to-person variation, but specific body sites in a given person tend to be stable over time.
Your gut microbiome at age twenty is meaningfully different from your roommate's, but yours at age twenty is mostly similar to yours at age twenty-five, barring antibiotics or major dietary changes.
Third, functional capacity is more conserved across people than species composition.
Two healthy people might have very different gut bacterial species but similar abilities to ferment dietary fiber, synthesize vitamins, and metabolize bile acids.
The redundancy in the system means many different species can do the same metabolic jobs.

Around the same time, a parallel European effort called MetaHIT — Metagenomics of the Human Intestinal Tract — was doing similar work focused specifically on the gut, with an emphasis on shotgun metagenomics from the start.
MetaHIT contributed enormously to the field, including an early and somewhat controversial idea called the enterotype hypothesis, proposed by Peer Bork's group in 2011.

The idea was that human gut microbiomes might fall into a small number of discrete clusters — three were proposed, dominated respectively by Bacteroides, Prevotella, and Ruminococcus — that you'd be sorted into based on long-term diet.
The enterotype concept caught fire in the popular press and was for a while sold as something like a microbiome blood type, with predictive value for diet and disease.

It hasn't held up well to scrutiny — most subsequent analyses find that gut microbiome variation is more continuous than discretely clustered — but the underlying observation, that long-term diet shapes gut microbiome composition in major ways, is solid.
A third large effort, this one citizen-science, deserves mention because of its scale and accessibility.

The American Gut Project, founded by Rob Knight at UC San Diego in 2012, took a crowdfunding approach: you paid about a hundred dollars, you got a stool sampling kit, you sent in your sample plus a detailed questionnaire about your diet and lifestyle, and they did 16S sequencing on it and sent you back a report on your gut microbiome.

By the mid-2010s, the American Gut Project had sampled over ten thousand people, making it the largest single dataset of human gut microbiomes ever assembled and the basis for many epidemiological analyses linking diet, lifestyle, and microbiome composition.

The democratized, opt-in design meant the cohort was biased toward health-conscious people willing to mail their poop to a lab in California, but the sheer scale produced robust findings about, for instance, the effects of dietary plant diversity on gut microbiome diversity.

The combined output of HMP, MetaHIT, American Gut, and the dozens of smaller studies of the early 2010s was a reference catalog — a baseline picture of what healthy human microbiomes look like, in different body sites, across different populations.
That catalog is what every subsequent study compares against.

When you read a paper that says "the gut microbiomes of patients with disease X were significantly different from healthy controls," there is, somewhere in the background, an HMP-era dataset defining what "healthy" looks like.
The catalog era was crucial.
It gave the field its ground.

It also revealed something humbling: even after thousands of healthy people had been sequenced, the genus-level taxa identified were limited and the functional core — the set of microbial functions present in essentially everyone — was clearer than any specific species core.
There is no single bacterial species that everyone has.

There are functions — fiber fermentation, short-chain fatty acid production, bile acid transformation — that almost everyone's gut community can perform, but the specific species doing those jobs varies from person to person.
Your microbiome is more like a guild of workers organized by trade than a fixed set of named individuals.
That metaphor — guild rather than census — has stuck, and it's a useful corrective when you read sensational claims about specific bacteria curing or causing diseases.
